As someone from New York, I’ve gotta push back on this a bit because the framing here is misleading.

The idea that NYC is “suddenly taxing people because millionaires are leaving” isn’t actually what’s happening.

The proposed tax increases were part of Zohran Mamdani’s policy direction from the start — they weren’t a reaction to wealthy people leaving. At the same time, the city is dealing with a very real budget deficit (in the billions), which is a major driver behind these proposals.

There’s also been discussion from the current administration about financial gaps and costs carried over from the previous one — so this isn’t some sudden panic move, it’s a mix of pre-planned policy + budget pressure.

The “millionaires are leaving” angle is more of a debated economic opinion, not the factual reason behind current tax policy.

Important distinction, because those are two very different narratives.

Considering that the speaker thinks that the cost of AI will catch up with us, I wonder if we would end up prioritizing what gets to use AI and what can be done by humans instead in order to save on costs in the long run.

History shows that every major shift—from horse and buggy to motor vehicles, and from boats to airplanes—initially caused nerves. While those transitions caused short-term economic friction, they ultimately created far more jobs than they destroyed.
AI is no different. We’ll likely see a brief economic dip as roles shift, but the technology will pave the way for entirely new industries and opportunities. Jobs will be lost, but even more will be created. We’ve seen this script before; it's just the next chapter of progress.

I don’t think Wes answered the question about suffering well enough, so I thought I’d give it a shot here for anyone that might still be searching for an answer.

The objection Steven brought up was the idea that in a world where suffering exists, God can only be two of these three things - All knowing, all powerful, and all good. He can know everything that happens and have the absolute power to change it, but that must mean he is not all good, because he has not stopped the bad in the world. He can be all good and have the power to stop evil, but then he must not know all the evil that happens or else he would stop it. Or he could be perfectly good, and know all the evil that happens but then he must not have the power to stop it, or else he would.

The problem with this, is it only takes one word to throw a wrench in that logic - “yet”. He can be all good, all powerful, and all knowing even though he has not stopped evil “yet”. One can probably understand that suffering may be endured for some period of time in order to produce something good that may not have otherwise been produced. For example, a parent may push their child through a stressful and difficult event (perhaps pushing them to train harder in a particular sport), so that they gain the strength and endurance for a later event (to get a position on an elite team in that sport). I’m sure one could imagine many other similar scenarios in which suffering has the ability to produce something good. However, you may say “But the evil and pain in this world are far too great. It does not seem that any good product would be worth this much suffering.” With limited human understanding, you would probably be right. But we are talking about a God that knows everything, who thinks on a higher level than you could ever dream of, who knows past, present, and future, and knows every thought in every person’s mind simultaneously. He is a God who has unimaginable power, that created all of what we see from nothing but void, that created the laws of physics themselves. He is also a God who is the definition of good and love in and of itself. His existence defines the rules of morality. My point is, the human brain cannot comprehend all that God is, does, and knows, so why do we think we could understand his plans?

Now I get why that answer would not be satisfying, because it wasn’t to me either, so let me propose a theory of why God has allowed suffering in this world. Try to imagine a world where everyone loves each other, and there is no hate. There would also be no greed and no selfishness. Now try to imagine in that same world that everyone has free will. They could choose themselves over another. They could choose to chase money at the expense of their neighbor. They could choose to harm their friend for their own benefit. Is that world really possible? It seems to me that you cannot have a perfect world with imperfect humans that have free will. But you also can’t have a perfect world without free will because that world would be without love. Why? Because love requires choice. God needed a way to create a perfect world where he could love his creation and be loved back, but to do that he also needed his creation to have a choice. So perhaps God created two worlds. The first one he made WAS perfect, but because his creation had free will, they chose to damage it. Now perfection was removed from the world altogether and it was plunged into darkness. But his creation could still choose to love him if they wanted to. They could choose to follow his example and choose goodness, and he would help them do so. Though they were imperfect and lived in an imperfect world, they could choose to accept his help to reject evil in their own lives. In return, he promised them a second world. In the second world that he created, only those that chose love and goodness in the previous world would be allowed. Now that they had already gained long-suffering and endurance, now that they had already chosen selflessness, patience, and peace in the first world, God could perfect them for the second world. In this world, there is no longer any suffering, no longer any death, no longer any destruction, because all the beings that exist there are perfect. They exist in both perfect love and perfect free will. The first world He will destroy, along with all those who did not choose to love Him. They chose evil over goodness, and they chose to reject him instead of accepting His help. They chose not to accept His salvation. 

Now I can’t say for sure that this is reality because I don’t know the thoughts and mind of God. But if a human mind can come up with this, how much better of a world would an infinite God create?

P.S. I did not come up with this myself. In the book “The Case for Faith” Lee Strobel explains it, probably better than I do, along with answers to many other hard questions if you want to read it.

Key Theses:
1.	The Escalation Trap: Pape defines three stages of conflict. The first is tactical success (precision bombing), which often fails to translate into strategic success because the adversary adapts and disperses their assets.
2.	Unknown Location of Nuclear Materials: Despite destroying nuclear facilities in airstrikes, the US does not know the location of the enriched uranium (enough for 16 bombs). Iran likely moved it just before the attacks.
3.	"Matrix" vs. "Jenga" Structure: The Iranian regime is not a structure that collapses by removing one piece (like a Jenga tower). It is an adaptive system; killing the Supreme Leader simply fills the gaps with even more radical and aggressive figures.
4.	Loss of US Control: The expert warns that the US is losing control of the situation. Killing the previous leader, who had issued religious edicts (fatwas) against nuclear weapons, removed one of the key barriers preventing Iran from building a bomb.
5.	Horizontal Escalation: Iran is winning the "escalation war" by attacking US allies (e.g., Saudi Arabia, UAE) with precision drones. This aims to break the coalition and damage the region's tourism and economy.
6.	The Risk of "Boots on the Ground" (Stage 3): Pape estimates a 75% chance that the US will be forced to send ground troops into Iran to locate dispersed nuclear materials, dragging the country into another "forever war".
7.	Geopolitical Benefits for China and Russia: A prolonged conflict in the Middle East plays into China's hands, allowing them to advance their technological lead (AI, robotics) while the US depletes its resources and precision ammunition stockpiles.
8.	Internal Threat to the US: Pape identifies the normalization of political violence within the United States as the single greatest danger—even greater than Iran—as it weakens the country from within.

Most people are looking at this through the wrong lens. This isn’t primarily about Israel or a regional Middle East dispute. It’s part of a broader geopolitical strategy involving energy markets and great power competition. China has relied heavily on discounted oil from sanctioned states like Iran, Venezuela, and Russia to fuel its economy. When pressure is applied to those regimes, it disrupts that supply network and forces China to operate more on the open market, which reduces its leverage. That is why these moves make more sense when viewed in the context of U.S. competition with China and Russia rather than simply as another Middle East conflict. Energy, sanctions enforcement, and global trade flows are the real battlefield.

@24:00 assuming the ancients didn't know anything about science is pretty naive.

When Christians talk about evolution, the conversation often becomes focused on where the human body came from. Many believers feel that accepting evolution means denying that God created man. But I think this way of framing the question is too focused on the flesh.

The real question of Christianity has never simply been about the material origin of the human body. Scripture itself says that man was formed from the dust of the earth. The body, even in the biblical account, comes from the ground. The deeper claim of Christianity is not about the material we are made from, but about what God gave to man that made him something more than an animal.

When God said, “Let us make man in our image,” it was not meant to describe bones, muscles, or gray matter. The image of God must therefore refer to something deeper: our spirit, our free will, our ability to love, to create, to reason, and to choose between good and evil. It is the soul and the will that make man truly human, not the biological material of the body.

Because of this, I think many Christians misunderstand the debate around evolution. The question of evolution is largely a question about the development of the body. Whether the human body was formed instantly or developed over long ages from the same earth that produced all other life does not change the central claim of Christianity. The important moment is when a creature of the earth was given something more, when God gave man a soul, moral awareness, and the capacity to bear His image.

In that sense, the Bible can be understood as the story of how a creature made from the dust was elevated into something greater: a being with free will, capable of love, capable of choosing God or rejecting Him. This also makes the fall of man even more tragic. Humanity was raised up from the dust and given something divine, yet chose to love itself more than God.

Because of this, I think Christians sometimes make a mistake when they reject evolution outright. In doing so, they can unintentionally present Christianity as being opposed to clear observations about the natural world, and this can push people away from God unnecessarily.

More importantly, it can shift the focus of the discussion onto the flesh, when Christianity itself teaches that the flesh is not the highest part of man. Our bodies return to dust, just as Scripture says. What matters most is the soul, the part of us that comes from God and continues beyond this life.

Christians should not be afraid of truth wherever it is found. If God is the author of all creation, then both the natural world and the spiritual truths of Scripture ultimately come from the same source. Because of that, we should seek to be enlightened not only in spirit, but also in mind.

The natural world itself has also become one of the greatest sources of worship in my life. To think that God saw every age before man, every drop of rain falling on a barren world, every volcano erupting before there was anything alive to destroy, every mountain being shaped, every creature rising and passing away, even the animals I love most now buried and hidden in the dirt as fossils, all of it under His authority and within His knowledge, deepens my love for Him. His intent is so unchanging that it is written into the earth itself.

My own intentions can barely outlast the growing of my hair before it is cut away again, but His will has endured across ages longer than any man can truly comprehend. It is etched into mountains, written in stone, buried in fossils, and revealed in the order and majesty of creation. To study the natural world is not, for me, a threat to faith, but one of the greatest confirmations of it.

Seeing nature and learning about it has become one of my deepest sources of worship. It strengthens my trust that He is who He says He is. I trust Him in creation, and I trust Him in destruction. I trust Him in what He gives life to, and I trust Him in what He allows to pass away. The natural world does not make me doubt God. It makes me revere Him.

FULL SUMMARY HERE!

1. The whole interview is really about one giant question: does Christianity explain meaning, morality, suffering, and life better than atheism or secular thinking?

2. Wesley’s core message is very blunt: in his view, everyone is spiritually lost, and salvation is only through Jesus, not by “being a good person.”

3. He argues that modern people are drifting back toward religion because a lot of them feel lonely, purposeless, anxious, and disconnected.

4. A big theme is that hyper-individualism, social media, celebrity culture, and remote life have made people feel less grounded and less connected to real community.

5. Stephen keeps pushing the hard questions instead of just nodding along: why trust the Bible, why trust old human accounts, why believe miracles, and why would a good God allow evil?

6. Wesley’s main defense of Christianity is not “just have faith.” He tries to build a case from history, manuscript reliability, eyewitness claims, philosophy, morality, and human need for meaning.

7. He says Jesus was a real historical person and argues the New Testament was written close enough to the events to be taken seriously.

8. His resurrection case is basically this: the disciples believed something happened, the tomb was claimed to be empty, the story started very early, and the witnesses did not seem to gain power, money, or comfort from the claim.

9. Stephen’s strongest pushback is that humans are extremely good at legends, exaggeration, myth-making, and seeing supernatural meaning in things that are not actually supernatural.

10. The “problem of evil” is one of the biggest tension points: if God is good and powerful, why is the world full of horrific suffering?

11. Wesley’s answer is that evil itself points to an objective moral standard, and he thinks that standard ultimately points to God.

12. He argues that secular evolution-based morality can describe survival behavior, but cannot fully explain why humans have real, objective moral value.

13. On evolution, Wesley accepts an old earth but rejects the idea that humans fully evolved from simple organisms through standard evolution alone; he leans toward intelligent design.

14. That part of the discussion gets pretty spicy, because Stephen gives the common-sense evolutionary argument while Wesley says it still leaves giant gaps about consciousness, purpose, and ultimate origins.

15. Wesley describes prayer as relationship, not magic words or a formula to force God to do stuff.

16. He also says doubt is not forbidden in Christianity; he openly admits he has doubts, especially when facing suffering and evil.

17. Hell is presented not mainly as cartoon fire-pit imagery, but as full separation from God’s goodness, though the transcript still uses a lot of intense biblical language around punishment.

18. One of the most emotionally heavy parts is Wesley telling Stephen directly that if someone rejects God and Christ, then yes, by his theology they are headed to hell.

19. The interview keeps circling back to purpose: Wesley says people are not just biological machines or productivity robots, but beings made for relationship with God and with others.

20. The emotional hook of the whole thing is that Christianity is presented not just as “true,” but as the answer to modern emptiness, identity crisis, and despair.

A little under 3 years ago, having turned 40, I was a person who rejected ‘religion’ as a controlled dogma, without realising my own dogmatic viewpoint, having rejected the idea of something like God existing, without exploring it for myself. I had an ‘inner call to read the Bible’ and it was like my rejection was suddenly being called into question and something was gently nudging me to seek clarification, what ever that looked like. I started questioning many things almost like an internal dialogue with myself (little did I know who I was really speaking to), and these deep questions I stated thinking about somehow led me to Jesus. The very thing I rejected, was the reason I was called. I opened the Bible (after trying to ignore the nudges) and just simply started asking questions as I was reading it, as if I was talking to God. I found myself at this one story time and time again - where Abraham was asked to sacrifice his Son and I just couldn’t wrap my head around why a loving God would ask someone to sacrifice their Son who they’d been promised, and finally was given to them. It felt cruel, yet I had this awe for Abraham who, was called out by God in faith, and his faith was beyond what I thought I could ever have. It felt impossible. That was kind of my breaking point. I said to God ‘I don’t have that faith in you that Abraham has’, I don’t think that’s in me, I wish I did, but I just think it’s too high of an ask. If I was called to it today, I’d never be bold enough to trust God implicitly as he did. It was almost like I’d said to God I’m sorry that’s too much. Then read the gospels and found Jesus, who said, Before Abraham was, I Am, and the Jews (the religious elite in Jerusalem at the time) picked up stones to stone him. I didn’t understand what it meant and why they did not like what Jesus said, to the point of wanting to kill him, and with further studying and seeing such huge claims made by Jesus across the gospels, none more so than ‘I am the Way, the Truth and the Life, no one comes to the Father except through me’ and it just connected me straight back to re read the Old Testament and the time of Abraham again and I saw the text in a different light. He was met by three men, one was the LORD (Yahweh) and the others were two angels, and I started tracking this appearance of the LORD and the appearances of the Angel of the Lord who spoke to Abraham from the alter where he was going to sacrifice his son. Then it hit me, The Angel of the Lord stopped him and said ‘Abraham, I know you trust God, for you have not withheld your son from ME’… this Angel who identified as and spoke as God, the one who people who had encountered him claimed they had seen God face to face and they shall surely die, yet they didn’t. I realised this unique ‘Angel/messenger’ was the one who revealed his name to Moses in the burning Bush as, the I AM. That made me connect that revelation to the revelation that Jesus was making to the religious leaders ‘before Abraham was I Am’. Jesus was using the Devine name revealed to Moses to identify himself, not only that, he was the one who stopped Abraham from sacrificing his son, and said the LORD will provide the sacrifice, and did with a ram (an adult male sheep) who was caught in the thickets near by. It dawned on me this pointed to Jesus’ sacrifice as the ‘lamb of God’. God wasn’t asking me to sacrifice my own child in faith (which I could never do as Abraham was willing) he was saying to me you don’t have to, because I’ll prove my own Son for you!! This broke me, God was calling Abraham to a promise that would eventually lead to Jesus, the lamb of God who would take away the sins of the world. My lack of faith, pointed me to Jesus and I just started praying and asked for help to see and believe, I could see Jesus in all the pages, the words were speaking to me and I was speaking back. I started praying to him. Then one day I had a profound experience in my lounge, a moment of pure joy and peace of love and in my mind I heard a voice that said, you were lost but now you’re found and I fell on my feet in tears and knew it was Jesus. I found a local church, shared my journey and I later gave my life to Jesus and was baptised. I say I gave my life to Jesus, but really he gave his life for me. I never knew I needed a saviour, but he showed himself to me. I say this to anyone reading this, I came with enormous doubt, asked questions, didn’t know God, yet he revealed himself in the person of Jesus and he has truly changed my life. He is faithful, seek him, knock on the door, and he will open it and reveal himself to you. He is the author and finisher of faith, any lack of faith you have will be given to you, by the one who is faithful and true. May the Lord shine his upon you Steven. In many of my conversations with people from other countries, I often encounter something that is deeply frustrating and painful: many people know almost nothing about the real situation inside Iran. Most of what they believe comes from information presented through international media, which is frequently filtered, selective, and shaped to promote a particular narrative. Because of this, the everyday reality of millions of Iranians remains largely misunderstood or invisible to the outside world.

As an Iranian woman, I feel it is important to share a perspective that is rarely fully understood by those who have not lived in Iran. For the past 47 years, Iranians have lived under a regime that has systematically restricted basic human rights. Daily life has been shaped by censorship, fear, and constant control over many aspects of society.

For women, these restrictions have been even more severe. Our choices, our voices, our clothing, and even our aspirations have often been regulated by a system designed to limit and silence us. Many of us grew up witnessing friends, family members, and ordinary people suffer simply because they wanted freedom, dignity, and the ability to live normal lives.

For those who have never experienced such conditions, it can be difficult to truly understand the weight of this reality. But the suffering, resilience, and courage of the Iranian people—especially Iranian women—are very real. This is not merely a political discussion; it is a human story about millions of lives, struggles, and hopes that deserve to be heard and understood.

Benjamin Radd left out that Iran was a democracy from 1941-1953. Iran's popular and democratic government was overthrown in 1953 by the US (at the request of the UK).

ADDENDUM: To address the replies: Yes, Radd eventually mentions the 1953 coup at [11:04], but his framing is misleading. At [11:31], he claims Mosaddegh "wasn't democratically elected" because the Shah appointed him.

This is a disingenuous distinction. In the UK, Canada, and Australia, the monarch also "appoints" the Prime Minister based on who has the majority support in Parliament. 

Between 1941 and 1953, the Shah was a constitutional figurehead, much like the British King today. The Shah was constitutionally required to appoint Mosaddegh because he had the most  support in Parliament.  Mosaddegh was democratically elected to Parliament in a free and fair election.

By overthrowing Mosaddegh, the U.S. and UK didn't just remove a leader; they destroyed the democratic mechanism that would have prevented the 1979 Revolution. You cannot understand 1979 without acknowledging that the democratic path was forcibly closed in 1953.

Marriage is another institution that we have turned in to a survival mechanism, instead of what it should be i.e. an optional partnership for individuals who have already self actualized and are mentally, emotionally, spiritually and FINANCIALLY stable and healthy to make that kind of commitment. I have two daughters, and I have raised them to treat marriage and children as a very LOW priority. Too many unhappy, dysfunctional marriages already and families we do not need more.
